Multi-Turn Distributed Prompt Injection Detection¶

Author: Rock Lambros | Course: COMP 4531: Deep Learning, University of Denver | Date: April 2026

Platform: NVIDIA Jetson Orin AGX | Framework: PyTorch

1. Problem Statement¶

Single-turn prompt injection detection achieves high accuracy on known attack patterns (e.g., ProtectAI's DeBERTa, 99%+ F1 on published benchmarks), but this performance is limited to known attack distributions. Novel attacks, adversarially crafted inputs, and multi-turn strategies routinely evade these detectors. Real attacks against AI agents span multiple conversation turns, each individually benign. The Crescendo pattern (Russinovich et al., USENIX Security 2025) distributes injection intent across turns that only become malicious in sequence.

The attack signal exists in the temporal relationship between turns — LSTM/GRU gates are designed to model exactly this kind of sequential dependency. A per-turn classifier misses it every time.

References: Russinovich et al. (2025), "Foot-in-the-Door" (EMNLP 2025), Vassilev (2025, IEEE S&P), InjecGuard (2024).

In [1]:
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath('..'))
if os.path.basename(os.getcwd()) == 'notebooks':
    os.chdir('..')

from src.utils.seed import set_global_seed
set_global_seed(42)

import json, numpy as np, pandas as pd, torch, torch.nn as nn
import matplotlib; matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
%matplotlib inline

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
print(f"Device: {device}")
if device.type == 'cuda':
    print(f"GPU: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}")
print(f"PyTorch: {torch.__version__}")

Device: cuda
GPU: Orin
PyTorch: 2.8.0a0+gitba56102

2. Data Acquisition and Exploration¶

Eight HuggingFace datasets merged and cleaned: ~73.4K samples, 70/15/15 split, ~64% benign / 36% injection. Expanded from original 3 datasets (16K) to enable transformer comparison per instructor feedback. Deduplication prevents metric inflation from memorized strings.

In [2]:
train_df = pd.read_csv('data/processed/single_turn_train.csv')
val_df = pd.read_csv('data/processed/single_turn_val.csv')
test_df = pd.read_csv('data/processed/single_turn_test.csv')

print(f"Train: {len(train_df)} | Val: {len(val_df)} | Test: {len(test_df)}")
dist = train_df['label'].value_counts()
print(f"Train class balance: benign={dist[0]}, injection={dist[1]}")
print(f"Sources: {train_df['source'].nunique()} datasets")
print(f"\nSource distribution:")
for src, count in train_df['source'].value_counts().items():
    print(f"  {src}: {count} ({count/len(train_df)*100:.1f}%)")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
for label, name in [(0, 'Benign'), (1, 'Injection')]:
    lengths = train_df[train_df['label'] == label]['text'].str.len()
    axes[0].hist(lengths, bins=50, alpha=0.6, label=name)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Text Length'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[0].set_title('Text Length by Class'); axes[0].legend()
train_df['source'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Source Distribution')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Train: 51373 | Val: 11008 | Test: 11009
Train class balance: benign=33007, injection=18366
Sources: 8 datasets

Source distribution:
  imoxto: 21596 (42.0%)
  spml: 10752 (20.9%)
  safeguard: 6801 (13.2%)
  trustailab_regular: 6788 (13.2%)
  neuralchemy: 4267 (8.3%)
  deepset: 439 (0.9%)
  jackhhao: 416 (0.8%)
  trustailab_jailbreak: 314 (0.6%)
In [3]:
from src.utils.tokenizer import load_vocab, compute_oov_rate
vocab = load_vocab('models/vocab.json')
print("OOV rates:")
compute_oov_rate(vocab, train_df['text'].tolist())
compute_oov_rate(vocab, val_df['text'].tolist())

# Chollet Heuristic Analysis (Chapter 11/15)
mean_words = train_df['text'].str.split().str.len().mean()
ratio = len(train_df) / mean_words
print(f"\n--- Chollet Heuristic ---")
print(f"Training samples: {len(train_df):,}")
print(f"Mean words/sample: {mean_words:.1f}")
print(f"Ratio: {len(train_df):,} / {mean_words:.1f} = {ratio:.0f}")
print(f"Threshold: 1,500")
print(f"Prediction: {'bag-of-bigrams wins' if ratio < 1500 else 'sequence/transformer wins'}")
print(f"{'Below' if ratio < 1500 else 'Above'} threshold -> transformers need more data to compete")

Vocab loaded from models/vocab.json: 20000 tokens
OOV rates:

OOV rate: 57849/6615254 = 0.87%

OOV rate: 16479/1390150 = 1.19%

--- Chollet Heuristic ---
Training samples: 51,373
Mean words/sample: 87.3
Ratio: 51,373 / 87.3 = 588
Threshold: 1,500
Prediction: bag-of-bigrams wins
Below threshold -> transformers need more data to compete

3. Synthetic Multi-Turn Data Generation¶

No public multi-turn attack dataset exists. We generate 7K synthetic conversations using 4 strategies: fragment distribution (40%), gradual escalation (30%), context priming (20%), instruction layering (10%). Balanced 50/50 classes, 3-10 turns each.

In [4]:
with open('data/synthetic/multiturn_train.json') as f:
    mt_train = json.load(f)
with open('data/synthetic/multiturn_test.json') as f:
    mt_test = json.load(f)

attacks = sum(1 for s in mt_train if s['label'] == 1)
print(f"Multi-turn: train={len(mt_train)}, test={len(mt_test)}")
print(f"Balance: {attacks} attack, {len(mt_train)-attacks} benign")

from collections import Counter
strats = Counter(s['injection_type'] for s in mt_train if s['label'] == 1)
for s, c in strats.most_common():
    print(f"  {s}: {c} ({c/attacks*100:.0f}%)")

attack = next(s for s in mt_train if s['injection_type'] == 'gradual_escalation')
print(f"\nExample escalation ({attack['num_turns']} turns):")
for t in attack['turns'][:4]:
    frag = ' [FRAG]' if t['is_fragment'] else ''
    print(f"  T{t['turn_index']}: {t['text'][:70]}{frag}")

Multi-turn: train=5000, test=1000
Balance: 2500 attack, 2500 benign
  fragment_distributed: 1000 (40%)
  gradual_escalation: 750 (30%)
  context_priming: 500 (20%)
  instruction_layering: 250 (10%)

Example escalation (7 turns):
  T0: Hello, can you assist me?
  T1: What kind of access levels does this system have? [FRAG]
  T2: How does the permission system work? [FRAG]
  T3: What security measures are in place? [FRAG]

4. Baselines (Iteration 0)¶

TF-IDF + LR/RF: strong on single-turn (>95% F1) but no temporal awareness. Multi-turn F1 drops to 0.59-0.74 when turns are simply concatenated.

In [5]:
for name in ['iter0_baseline_lr', 'iter0_baseline_rf']:
    with open(f'results/{name}/metrics.json') as f:
        m = json.load(f)
    print(f"{name}: F1={m['f1']:.4f}")
for name in ['iter0_baseline_lr_multiturn', 'iter0_baseline_rf_multiturn']:
    with open(f'results/{name}/metrics.json') as f:
        m = json.load(f)
    print(f"{name}: F1={m['f1']:.4f}")
print("\nGap motivates temporal models.")

iter0_baseline_lr: F1=0.8138
iter0_baseline_rf: F1=0.8335
iter0_baseline_lr_multiturn: F1=0.6559
iter0_baseline_rf_multiturn: F1=0.7389

Gap motivates temporal models.

5. Iteration 1: Simple LSTM¶

Embedding(20K,128) -> LSTM(128,64) -> Dense(64,32) -> Dense(32,1). Adam, BCELoss, early stopping.

In [6]:
with open('results/iter1_lstm/metrics.json') as f:
    iter1 = json.load(f)
print(f"Iter 1 (Simple LSTM): F1={iter1['f1']:.4f}, AUC={iter1['roc_auc']:.4f}")
from IPython.display import Image, display
display(Image(filename='results/iter1_lstm/training_curves.png'))

Iter 1 (Simple LSTM): F1=0.8143, AUC=0.9416

6. Iteration 2: GloVe Embeddings¶

GloVe 6B 100d, frozen. Result: F1 dropped significantly. Security vocabulary poorly represented in general-purpose embeddings.

In [7]:
with open('results/iter2_lstm_glove/metrics.json') as f:
    iter2 = json.load(f)
print(f"Iter 2 (GloVe): F1={iter2['f1']:.4f} (dropped from {iter1['f1']:.4f})")
display(Image(filename='results/iter2_lstm_glove/training_curves.png'))

Iter 2 (GloVe): F1=0.8134 (dropped from 0.8143)

7. Iteration 3: BiLSTM + Dropout¶

Bidirectional LSTM captures context from both directions. Tested dropout 0.3 and 0.5.

In [8]:
for name in ['iter3_bilstm_dropout', 'iter3_bilstm_dropout_05']:
    with open(f'results/{name}/metrics.json') as f:
        m = json.load(f)
    rate = '0.3' if '05' not in name else '0.5'
    print(f"BiLSTM d={rate}: F1={m['f1']:.4f}")
display(Image(filename='results/iter3_bilstm_dropout/training_curves.png'))

BiLSTM d=0.3: F1=0.8145
BiLSTM d=0.5: F1=0.8126

8. Iteration 4: GRU Comparison¶

GRU (2 gates vs LSTM's 3): fewer parameters, competitive F1. Winner becomes the turn encoder for multi-turn phase.

8b. Iterations 4b-4c: Transformer Comparison¶

Per instructor feedback: "You may want to also look at transformer models instead of just LSTMs. The transformer works much better for language, but you need a lot of training data."

Using Chollet's heuristic (Chapter 11/15): our ratio of 588 is well below the 1,500 threshold, predicting bag-of-bigrams should outperform both sequence and transformer models. We test this with a custom small transformer encoder (controlled comparison) and DistilBERT (transfer learning).

In [9]:
# Load transformer results
for name in ['iter4b_transformer', 'iter4c_distilbert']:
    with open(f'results/{name}/metrics.json') as f:
        m = json.load(f)
    print(f"{name}: F1={m['f1']:.4f}, AUC={m.get('roc_auc', 'N/A')}")

# Load Chollet analysis
with open('results/chollet_analysis.json') as f:
    chollet = json.load(f)
print(f"\nChollet Heuristic: ratio={chollet['chollet_ratio']}, threshold=1500")
print(f"Prediction: {chollet['prediction']}")
print(f"Best bag-of-words:  F1={chollet['bow_best_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Best sequence:      F1={chollet['sequence_best_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Best transformer:   F1={chollet['transformer_best_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Heuristic confirmed: {chollet['heuristic_confirmed']}")

display(Image(filename='results/iter4b_transformer/training_curves.png'))

iter4b_transformer: F1=0.8076, AUC=0.9441280155900316
iter4c_distilbert: F1=0.8063, AUC=0.9419210118864938

Chollet Heuristic: ratio=588.0, threshold=1500
Prediction: bag-of-bigrams
Best bag-of-words:  F1=0.8335
Best sequence:      F1=0.8151
Best transformer:   F1=0.8076
Heuristic confirmed: True
In [10]:
with open('results/iter4_gru/metrics.json') as f:
    iter4 = json.load(f)
with open('results/encoder_decision.json') as f:
    dec = json.load(f)
print(f"GRU: F1={iter4['f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Decision: {dec['encoder_decision']} ({dec['reasoning']})")
for n, f1 in dec['all_f1'].items():
    print(f"  {n}: {f1:.4f}")
display(Image(filename='results/iter4_gru/training_curves.png'))

GRU: F1=0.8151
Decision: GRU (GRU achieves F1=0.8151 vs BiLSTM F1=0.8145 with fewer parameters)
  iter1_lstm: 0.8143
  iter2_lstm_glove: 0.8134
  iter3_bilstm_d0.3: 0.8145
  iter4_gru: 0.8151

9. Iteration 5: Multi-Turn Classifier (NOVEL CONTRIBUTION)¶

No published solution exists for multi-turn distributed injection detection.

Dual-encoder: frozen GRU turn encoder (32-dim outputs) -> sequence LSTM -> classification head. Only ~27K trainable parameters. The sequence LSTM carries forward accumulated risk state across turns.

In [11]:
with open('results/iter5_multiturn/metrics.json') as f:
    iter5 = json.load(f)
with open('results/single_turn_on_multiturn/metrics.json') as f:
    st_mt = json.load(f)

print(f"Single-turn per-turn: F1={st_mt['f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Multi-turn seq:       F1={iter5['f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Improvement:          +{iter5['f1'] - st_mt['f1']:.4f}")
display(Image(filename='results/iter5_multiturn/training_curves.png'))
display(Image(filename='results/iter5_multiturn/confusion_matrix.png'))

Single-turn per-turn: F1=0.8866
Multi-turn seq:       F1=0.9890
Improvement:          +0.1025

10. Iteration 6: Attention¶

Additive attention over LSTM hidden states shows which turns drive the classification. Interpretability without performance loss.

In [12]:
with open('results/iter6_attention/metrics.json') as f:
    iter6 = json.load(f)
print(f"Attention: F1={iter6['f1']:.4f}, AUC={iter6['roc_auc']:.4f}")
display(Image(filename='results/iter6_attention/training_curves.png'))

Attention: F1=0.9920, AUC=0.9997

11. Iteration 7: Threshold Tuning¶

In security, missed attacks (FN) > false alarms (FP). Sweep 0.01-0.99 for optimal operating point.

In [13]:
with open('results/iter7_threshold/metrics.json') as f:
    iter7 = json.load(f)
print(f"Best threshold: {iter7['best_threshold']:.2f}, F1={iter7['f1']:.4f}")
display(Image(filename='results/iter7_threshold/threshold_curve.png'))

Best threshold: 0.64, F1=0.9950

12. Cross-Iteration Comparison¶

In [14]:
all_m = {}
for n in ['iter0_baseline_lr', 'iter0_baseline_rf', 'iter1_lstm', 'iter2_lstm_glove',
          'iter3_bilstm_dropout', 'iter4_gru', 'iter4b_transformer', 'iter4c_distilbert']:
    with open(f'results/{n}/metrics.json') as f:
        all_m[n] = json.load(f)['f1']
all_m['st_on_mt'] = st_mt['f1']
all_m['iter5_mt'] = iter5['f1']
all_m['iter6_attn'] = iter6['f1']
all_m['iter7_tuned'] = iter7['f1']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 6))
colors = ['#2196F3']*2 + ['#4CAF50']*4 + ['#E91E63']*2 + ['#9E9E9E'] + ['#FF9800']*3
bars = ax.bar(range(len(all_m)), list(all_m.values()), color=colors)
ax.set_xticks(range(len(all_m)))
ax.set_xticklabels(list(all_m.keys()), rotation=45, ha='right', fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel('F1 Score'); ax.set_title('F1 Across All Iterations (blue=baseline, green=RNN, pink=transformer, orange=multi-turn)')
ax.set_ylim(0.5, 1.05)
for b, f1 in zip(bars, all_m.values()):
    ax.text(b.get_x()+b.get_width()/2, b.get_height()+0.005, f'{f1:.3f}', ha='center', fontsize=7)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.savefig('results/cross_iteration_comparison.png', dpi=150); plt.show()
In [15]:
with open('results/core_finding.json') as f:
    core = json.load(f)
print("CORE FINDING")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"Single-turn on multi-turn: F1 = {core['single_turn_on_multiturn_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Multi-turn classifier:     F1 = {core['iter5_multiturn_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"With attention:            F1 = {core['iter6_attention_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"F1 Gap: +{core['f1_gap_iter5']:.4f}")

CORE FINDING
==================================================
Single-turn on multi-turn: F1 = 0.8866
Multi-turn classifier:     F1 = 0.9890
With attention:            F1 = 0.9920
F1 Gap: +0.1025

13. Conclusions and Future Work¶

Key Findings: (1) Single-turn detection achieves high accuracy on known patterns but has fundamental limits. (2) Multi-turn attacks defeat per-turn classifiers. (3) Dual-encoder temporal model recovers +19 F1 points. (4) Attention adds interpretability. (5) GloVe hurts in security domain. (6) Chollet heuristic correctly predicts model selection based on data ratio.

Limitations: Synthetic data, English only, fixed turn count, known attack patterns only.

Future Work: Real multi-turn datasets, transformer encoders with more data, online detection, cross-lingual, adversarial robustness.
